Essa extensão, ela foi criada por um desenvolvedor independente e o código dela é totalmente aberto lá no GitHub, ou seja, qualquer pessoa pode ler toda a sua documentação antes de instalar. Essa ferramenta adiciona três funções diretamente ali na interface do chat. A primeira função é a contagem de tokens dentro daquela conversa. A extensão exibe uma estimativa de tokens que você pode verificar dentro da mesma interface do chat. É uma barra de contagem até o limite de 200.000 tokens que é a janela de contexto atual dentro do cloud. Então, para tarefas mais importantes que você precisa que o modelo esteja 100% funcional para você, vale você respeitar esse limite e levar para um outro chat mais leve, respeitando esse limite, especialmente em tarefas mais importantes que você precisa do modelo ali 100% trabalhando com você. A segunda função que ela adiciona, ela é um contador do cash. Essa função você não tem no cloud, nem de forma nativa, você não tem em nenhum outro lugar. Esse contador ele começa a partir do momento que você manda uma mensagem, ele começa a fazer uma contagem de 5 minutos onde o cash ainda está carregado. The problem that many people are describing is not having a limit per se. Everyone understands this.  Chat GPT is available, Clou is available.  Now Gemini adopts the same logic.  The problem was the element of surprise.  If you opened Gemini in the last few days, you may have noticed that you sent some messages and the notification already appeared.  You have reached your daily creation limit.  So, know that it wasn't just you.  For those who use Gemini occasionally, this might not be a problem, but for those who actually work with the tool within a real process to analyze articles, compile reports, or conduct in- depth research, this blockage can appear in a few minutes or in less than an hour.  Writer's block isn't just about waiting a few hours; it's about losing the thread of the analysis, having to restart that entire line of reasoning from where you left off. Until recently, the profile of those who used AI was very simple.  You had a limit on the number of messages you could send: 40 messages per session, 50 messages per session.  It wasn't a perfect method, but at least it was predictable; you could plan ahead.  Google changed that way of working.  The question is no longer how many messages you sent, but rather how much processing each message achieved. To the average user, this change seems like just a technical detail.  In practice, it completely changes the logic of its use.  A short message may seem inexpensive, but another equally short message may be much more expensive.  This depends on how much processing each message will require from the model.  It also depends on what's inside that conversation, whether the more robust model is enabled, how many files you sent, and what features you're using.  The problem is that you only find out the cost after you've already sent the message, not before. The best way for you to understand how this processing format works is to imagine two different usage modes.  First, you send a simple question, for example, explain this concept.  Give me an idea for a title, summarize it in a paragraph.  The chat is light within this context.  You haven't sent many messages; you're only having a conversation with the chat.  And a second way to use it, similarly you send few messages, but within that message there's a large PDF, there's a data analysis that you requested and a cross-referencing to do with that data and that PDF.  In terms of who used this second method, it also seems like a small number; there were very few messages.  On the server's account, he used it a lot, perhaps much more than someone who sent 40 or 50 messages.  And that's where the frustration arises; the person feels penalized for using AI in the most productive way, for real work, to send files they want to analyze.  In my understanding, especially for Gemini users, the problem isn't charging for processing, but rather not making that clear before you send each message.  If each task has a different weight, the user needed to know how much they would spend.  Once the chat is blocked, the operation freezes, and this information is no longer useful. There's one point that still doesn't come up much in discussions about the subject.  In chat, it's not just that last message you send.  When you continue a long conversation, the model needs to process everything before it comes along.  All the files you sent, all that context, it needs to process in each new message.  to preserve that context and the coherence in the answer he will give you.  Therefore, a short question within a heavily loaded chat ceases to be lightweight and starts consuming a lot of server resources.  You write a summary now; for you it's just a sentence, but for the model, it needs to access that sentence, the PDF you submitted, all the answers you submitted previously, all those corrections, instructions—everything needs to be processed together for that answer to make sense.  When you integrate this with more complex tools, such as deep research, image generation, and video analysis within the same chat, the cost increases even further.  Not because you clicked on something different, but because each of these messages needs to process all these tools together in a single response.  So, even if you only sent a simple text message, they are all grouped together within one interface.  It seems like you're using the chat in a regular way and just adding a few tools in Gemini, but in reality, all of that—the cost of the context and the cost of the tools— greatly increases the processing that the servers will have to do, and also increases token consumption.
